## **Video Diffusion Models**

**Jonathan Ho** _[∗]_ `jonathanho@google.com`

**Tim Salimans** _[∗]_ **Alexey Gritsenko** `salimans@google.com agritsenko@google.com`

**William Chan** `williamchan@google.com`

**Mohammad Norouzi David J. Fleet** `mnorouzi@google.com davidfleet@google.com`

## **Abstract**

Generating temporally coherent high fidelity video is an important milestone in generative modeling research. We make progress towards this milestone by proposing a diffusion model for video generation that shows very promising initial results. Our model is a natural extension of the standard image diffusion architecture, and it enables jointly training from image and video data, which we find to reduce the variance of minibatch gradients and speed up optimization. To generate long and higher resolution videos we introduce a new conditional sampling technique for spatial and temporal video extension that performs better than previously proposed methods. We present the first results on a large text-conditioned video generation task, as well as state-of-the-art results on established benchmarks for video prediction and unconditional video generation. Supplementary material is available at `https://video-diffusion.github.io/` .

## **1 Introduction**

Diffusion models have recently been producing high quality results in image generation and audio generation [e.g. 28, 39, 40, 16, 23, 36, 48, 60, 42, 10, 29], and there is significant interest in validating diffusion models in new data modalities. In this work, we present first results on video generation using diffusion models, for both unconditional and conditional settings.

We show that high quality videos can be generated using essentially the standard formulation of the Gaussian diffusion model [46], with little modification other than straightforward architectural changes to accommodate video data within the memory constraints of deep learning accelerators. We train models that generate a fixed number of video frames using a 3D U-Net diffusion model architecture, and we enable generating longer videos by applying this model autoregressively using a new method for conditional generation. We additionally show the benefits of joint training on video and image modeling objectives. We test our methods on video prediction and unconditional video generation, where we achieve state-of-the-art sample quality scores, and we also show promising first results on text-conditioned video generation.

## **3 Video diffusion models**

Our approach to video generation using diffusion models is to use the standard diffusion model formalism described in Section 2 with a neural network architecture suitable for video data. Each of our models is trained to jointly model a fixed number of frames at a fixed spatial resolution. To extend sampling to longer sequences of frames or higher spatial resolutions, we will repurpose our models with a conditioning technique described later in Section 3.1.

In prior work on image modeling, the standard architecture for **x** ˆ _θ_ in an image diffusion model is a U-Net [38, 44], which is a neural network architecture constructed as a spatial downsampling pass followed by a spatial upsampling pass with skip connections to the downsampling pass activations. The network is built from layers of 2D convolutional residual blocks, for example in the style of the Wide ResNet [65], and each such convolutional block is followed by a spatial attention block [55, 58, 11]. Conditioning information, such as **c** and _λt_ , is provided to the network in the form of an embedding vector added into each residual block (we find it helpful for our models to process these embedding vectors using several MLP layers before adding).

We propose to extend this image diffusion model architecture to video data, given by a block of a fixed number of frames, using a particular type of 3D U-Net [13] that is factorized over space and time. First, we modify the image model architecture by changing each 2D convolution into a space-only 3D convolution, for instance, we change each 3x3 convolution into a 1x3x3 convolution (the first axis indexes video frames, the second and third index the spatial height and width). The attention in each spatial attention block remains as attention over space; i.e., the first axis is treated as a batch axis. Second, after each spatial attention block, we insert a temporal attention block that performs attention over the first axis and treats the spatial axes as batch axes. We use relative position embeddings [45] in each temporal attention block so that the network can distinguish ordering of frames in a way that does not require an absolute notion of video time. We visualize the model architecture in Fig. 1.

Figure 1: The 3D U-Net architecture for **x** ˆ _θ_ in the diffusion model. Each block represents a 4D tensor with axes labeled as frames _×_ height _×_ width _×_ channels, processed in a space-time factorized manner as described in Section 3. The input is a noisy video **z** _t_ , conditioning **c** , and the log SNR _λt_ . The downsampling/upsampling blocks adjust the spatial input resolution height _×_ width by a factor of 2 through each of the _K_ blocks. The channel counts are specified using channel multipliers _M_ 1, _M_ 2, ..., _MK_ , and the upsampling pass has concatenation skip connections to the downsampling pass.

The use of factorized space-time attention is known to be a good choice in video transformers for its computational efficiency [2, 5, 21]. An advantage of our factorized space-time architecture, which is unique to our video generation setting, is that it is particularly straightforward to mask the model to run on independent images rather than a video, simply by removing the attention operation inside each time attention block and fixing the attention matrix to exactly match each key and query vector at each video timestep. The utility of doing so is that it allows us to jointly train the model on both video and image generation. We find in our experiments that this joint training is important for sample quality (Section 4).

3

## **3.1 Reconstruction-guided sampling for improved conditional generation**

The videos we consider modeling typically consist of hundreds to thousands of frames, at a frame rate of at least 24 frames per second. To manage the computational requirements of training our models, we only train on a small subset of say 16 frames at a time. However, at test time we can generate longer videos by extending our samples. For example, we could first generate a video **x**[a] _∼ pθ_ ( **x** ) consisting of 16 frames, and then extend it with a second sample **x**[b] _∼ pθ_ ( **x**[b] _|_ **x**[a] ). If **x**[b] consists of frames following **x**[a] , this allows us to autoregressively extend our sampled videos to arbitrary lengths, which we demonstrate in Section 4.3.3. Alternatively, we could choose **x**[a] to represent a video of lower frame rate, and then define **x**[b] to be those frames in between the frames of **x**[a] . This allows one to then to upsample a video temporally, similar to how [34] generate high resolution images through spatial upsampling.

Both approaches require one to sample from a conditional model, _pθ_ ( **x**[b] _|_ **x**[a] ). This conditional model could be trained explicitly, but it can also be derived approximately from our unconditional model _pθ_ ( **x** ) by imputation, which has the advantage of not requiring a separately trained model. The additional gradient term in this expression can be interpreted as a form of _guidance_ [16, 20] based on the model’s reconstruction of the conditioning data, and we therefore refer to this method as _reconstruction-guided sampling_ , or simply _reconstruction guidance_ . Like with other forms of guidance, we find that choosing a larger weighting factor, _wr >_ 1, tends to improve sample quality. We empirically investigate reconstruction guidance in Section 4.3.3, where we find it to work surprisingly well, especially when combined with predictor-corrector samplers using Langevin diffusion [48].

Reconstruction guidance also extends to the case of spatial interpolation (or super-resolution), in which the mean squared error loss is imposed on a downsampled version of the model prediction, and backpropagation is performed through this downsampling. ## **4 Experiments**

We report our results on video diffusion models for unconditional video generation (Section 4.1), conditional video generation (video prediction) (Section 4.2), and text-conditioned video generation (Section 4.3). We evaluate our models using standard metrics such as FVD [54], FID [19], and IS [43]; details on evaluation are provided below alongside each benchmark. Samples and additional results are provided at `https://video-diffusion.github.io/` . Architecture hyperparameters, training details, and compute resources are listed in Appendix A.

## **4.1 Unconditional video modeling**

To demonstrate our approach on unconditional generation, we use a popular benchmark of Soomro et al. [49] for unconditional modeling of video. The benchmark consists of short clips of people performing one of 101 activities, and was originally collected for the purpose of training action recognition models. We model short segments of 16 frames from this dataset, downsampled to a spatial resolution of 64x64. In Table 1 we present perceptual quality scores for videos generated by our model, and we compare against methods from the literature, finding that our method strongly improves upon the previous state-of-the-art.

We use the data loader provided by TensorFlow Datasets [1] without further processing, and we train on all 13,320 videos. Similar to previous methods, we use the C3D network [51][2] for calculating FID and IS, using 10,000 samples generated from our model. C3D internally resizes input data to the 112x112 spatial resolution, so perceptual scores are approximately comparable even when the data is sampled at a different resolution originally. As discussed by [64], methods in the literature are unfortunately not always consistent in the data preprocessing that is used, which may lead to small differences in reported scores between papers. The Inception Score we calculate for real data ( _≈_ 60) is consistent with that reported by [26], who also report a higher real data Inception score of _≈_ 90 for data sampled at the 128x128 resolution, which indicates that our 64x64 model might be at a disadvantage compared to works that generate at a higher resolution. Nevertheless, our model obtains the best perceptual quality metrics that we could find in the literature.

## **4.2 Video prediction**

A common benchmark task for evaluating generative models of video is _video prediction_ , where the model is given the first frame(s) of a video and is asked to generate the remainder. Models that do well on this _conditional generation_ task are usually trained explicitly for this conditional setting, for example by being autoregressive across frames. Although our models are instead only trained unconditionally, we can adapt them to the video prediction setting by using the guidance method proposed in section 3.1. Here we evaluate this method on two popular video prediction benchmarks, obtaining state-of-the-art results.

**BAIR Robot Pushing** We evaluate video prediction performance on BAIR Robot Pushing [17], a standard benchmark in the video literature consisting of approximately 44000 videos of robot pushing motions at the 64x64 spatial resolution. Methods for this benchmark are conditioned on 1 frame and generate the next 15. Results are listed in Table 2. Following the evaluation protocol of [4] and others, we calculate FVD [54] using the I3D network [8] by comparing 100 _×_ 256 model samples against the 256 examples in the evaluation set.

**Kinetics-600** We additionally evaluate video prediction performance on the Kinetics-600 benchmark [27, 9]. Kinetics-600 contains approximately 400 thousand training videos depicting 600 different activities. We train unconditional models on this dataset at the 64 _×_ 64 resolution and evaluate on 50 thousand randomly sampled videos from the test set, where we condition on a randomly sampled subsequence of 5 frames and generate the next 11 frames. Like previous works, we calculate FVD and Inception Score using the I3D network [8]. See Table 3 for results. In our reported results we sample test videos without replacement, and we use the same randomly selected subsequences for generating model samples and for defining the ground truth, since this results in the lowest bias and variance in the reported FVD metric. However, from personal communication we learned that [33, 14] instead sampled _with replacement_ , and used a different random seed when sampling the ground truth data. We find that this way of evaluating raises the FVD obtained by our model slightly, from 16 _._ 2 to 16 _._ 9. Inception Score is unaffected.

## **4.3 Text-conditioned video generation**

The remaining experiments reported are on text-conditioned video generation. In this text-conditioned video generation setting, we employ a dataset of 10 million captioned videos, and we condition the diffusion model on captions in the form of BERT-large embeddings [15] processed using attention pooling. We consider two model sizes: a small model for the joint training ablation, and a large model for generating the remaining results (both architectures are described in detail in Appendix A), and we explore the effects of joint video-image training, classifier-free guidance, and our newly proposed reconstruction guidance method for autoregressive extension and simultaneous spatial and temporal super-resolution. We report the following metrics in this section on 4096 samples: the video metric FVD, and the Inception-based image metrics FID and IS measured by averaging activations across frames (FID/IS-avg) and by measuring the first frame only (FID/IS-first). For FID and FVD, we report two numbers which are measured against the training and validation sets, respectively. For IS, we report two numbers which are averaged scores across 1 split and 10 splits of samples, respectively.

## **4.3.1 Joint training on video and image modeling**

As described in Section 3, one of the main advantages of our video architecture is that it allows us to easily train the model jointly on video and image generative modeling objectives. To implement this joint training, we concatenate random independent image frames to the end of each video sampled from the dataset, and we mask the attention in the temporal attention blocks to prevent mixing information across video frames and each individual image frame. We choose these random independent images from random videos within the same dataset; in future work we plan to explore the effect of choosing images from other larger image-only datasets.

Table 4 reports results for an experiment on text-conditioned 16x64x64 videos, where we consider training on an additional 0, 4, or 8 independent image frames per video. One can see clear improvements in video and image sample quality metrics as more independent image frames are added. Adding independent image frames has the effect of reducing variance of the gradient at the expense of some bias for the video modeling objective, and thus it can be seen as a memory optimization to fit more independent examples in a batch.

## **4.3.2 Effect of classifier-free guidance**

Table 5 reports results that verify the effectiveness of classifier-free guidance [20] on text-to-video generation. As expected, there is clear improvement in the Inception Score-like metrics with higher guidance weight, while the FID-like metrics improve and then degrade with increasing guidance weight. Similar findings have been reported on text-to-image generation [36].

7

Figure 3 shows the effect of classifier-free guidance [20] on a text-conditioned video model. Similar to what was observed in other work that used classifier-free guidance on text-conditioned image generation [36] and class-conditioned image generation [20, 16], adding guidance increases the sample fidelity of each individual image and emphases the effect of the conditioning signal.

## **4.3.3 Autoregressive video extension for longer sequences**

In Section 3.1 we proposed the _reconstruction guidance method_ for conditional sampling from diffusion models, an improvement over the _replacement method_ of [48]. In Table 6 we present results on generating longer videos using both techniques, and find that our proposed method indeed improves over the replacement method in terms of perceptual quality scores.

Figure 4 shows the samples of our reconstruction guidance method for conditional sampling compared to the replacement method (Section 3.1) for the purposes of generating long samples in a blockautoregressive manner (Section 4.3.3). The samples from the replacement method clearly show a lack of temporal coherence, since frames from different blocks throughout the generated videos appear to be uncorrelated samples (conditioned on **c** ). The samples from the reconstruction guidance method, by contrast, are clearly temporally coherent over the course of the entire autoregressive generation process. Figure 2 additionally shows samples of using the reconstruction guidance method to simultaneously condition on low frequency, low resolution videos while autoregressively extending temporally at a high resolution.

8

Figure 4: Comparing the replacement method (left) vs the reconstruction guidance method (right) for conditioning for block-autoregressive generation of 64 frames from a 16 frame model. Video frames are displayed over time from left to right; each row is an independent sample. The replacement method suffers from a lack of temporal coherence, unlike the reconstruction guidance method.

## **5 Related work**

Prior work on video generation has usually employed other types of generative models, notably, autoregressive models, VAEs, GANs, and normalizing flows [e.g. 3, 4, 32, 30, 14, 59, 62, 57]. Related work on model classes similar to diffusion models includes [25, 24]. Concurrent work [63] proposes a diffusion-based approach to video generation that uses an image diffusion model to predict each individual frame within a RNN temporal autoregressive model. Our video diffusion model, by contrast, jointly models entire videos (blocks of frames) using a 3D video architecture with interleaved spatial and temporal attention, and we extend to long sequence lengths by filling in frames or autoregressive temporal extension.

## **6 Conclusion**

We have introduced diffusion models for video modeling, thus bringing recent advances in generative modeling using diffusion models to the video domain. We have shown that with straightforward extensions of conventional U-Net architectures for 2D image modeling to 3D space-time, with factorized space-time attention blocks, one can learn effective generative models for video data using the standard formulation of the diffusion model. This includes unconditional models, text-conditioned models, and video prediction models.

We have additionally demonstrated the benefits of joint image-video training and classifier-free guidance for video diffusion models on both video and image sample quality metrics, and we also introduced a new reconstruction-guided conditional sampling method that outperforms existing replacement or imputation methods for conditional sampling from unconditionally trained models. Our reconstruction guidance method can generate long sequences using either frame interpolation (or temporal super-resolution) or extrapolation in an auto-regressive fashion, and also can perform spatial super-resolution. We look forward to investigating this method in a wider variety of conditioning settings.

Our goal with this work is to advance research on methods in generative modeling, and our methods have the potential to positively impact creative downstream applications. As with prior work in generative modeling, however, our methods have the potential for causing harmful impact and could enhance malicious or unethical uses of generative models, such as fake content generation, harassment, and misinformation spread, and thus we have decided not to release our models. Like all generative models, our models reflect the biases of their training datasets and thus may require curation to ensure fair results from sampling. In particular, our text-to-video models inherit the challenges faced by prior work on text-to-image models, and our future work will involve auditing for forms of social bias, similar to [6, 7, 50, 12] for image-to-text and image labeling models. We see our work as only a starting point for further investigation on video diffusion models and investigation into their societal implications, and we will aim to explore benchmark evaluations for social and cultural bias in the video generation setting and make the necessary research advances to address them.

coarse__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview.label: Coarse (gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview)
coarse__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview.overall_feedback:
This paper proposes a 3D U-Net diffusion model for video generation, utilizing factorized space-time attention and a novel reconstruction-guided sampling technique for temporal extension.

The paper provides a strong empirical foundation for applying diffusion models to video, demonstrating state-of-the-art results on several benchmarks. The proposed reconstruction guidance effectively addresses the challenge of temporal coherence in autoregressive generation, making it a significant contribution to the field.

Overview issues:

- Ambiguity in Evaluation Metrics: The paper relies heavily on FVD and IS, which are known to be problematic for assessing temporal consistency and motion realism. While the authors acknowledge some limitations, they do not provide a qualitative or quantitative analysis of how their model handles specific motion dynamics, such as object persistence or camera motion. Given that FVD is an image-based metric computed on frame sequences, it fails to capture high-frequency flickering or temporal drift. The revision should include a human evaluation study or a more robust metric (like a motion-aware score) to validate the temporal coherence claim.

- Lack of Ablation on Factorized Attention: The authors propose a 3D U-Net with factorized space-time attention, yet they do not provide an ablation study comparing this architecture against a non-factorized baseline or a standard 2D U-Net with temporal layers. It is unclear if the performance gains are due to the diffusion framework itself or the specific architectural choices for temporal modeling. To strengthen the contribution, the authors should provide a comparison that isolates the impact of the temporal attention blocks versus the spatial-only baseline.

- Incomplete Discussion of Computational Scaling: Section 3 describes a 3D U-Net architecture, but the paper lacks a detailed discussion on the memory scaling and inference latency for longer video sequences. As the number of frames increases, the computational cost of the temporal attention mechanism grows quadratically. The paper would benefit from a complexity analysis or a discussion on the practical limits of the autoregressive extension method, particularly regarding how drift accumulates over long sequences.

- Sensitivity of Reconstruction Guidance: Equation 7 introduces the reconstruction guidance method with a weighting factor w_r. The paper demonstrates that this method improves coherence, but it does not provide an analysis of the sensitivity of the results to the choice of w_r. If the method is highly sensitive to this hyperparameter, it limits its generalizability to different datasets or motion types. The authors should provide a plot or table showing the impact of varying w_r on FVD scores to establish the robustness of the proposed sampling technique.

- Absence of quantitative analysis on temporal drift and error accumulation: The paper claims that the reconstruction-guided sampling allows for autoregressive extension of video sequences, yet it lacks a quantitative assessment of how generation quality degrades over time. Autoregressive models are notoriously prone to error accumulation and temporal drift, which can manifest as scene content degradation or motion artifacts after several iterations. To substantiate the claim that this method is effective for long-sequence generation, the authors should provide a plot of FVD or a similar metric as a function of sequence length (e.g., 16, 32, 64, 128 frames) to demonstrate the stability of the autoregressive process.

- Lack of empirical validation for joint image-video training benefits: The authors argue that joint training on image and video data reduces gradient variance and accelerates optimization, but they do not provide a direct comparison of the convergence behavior between a model trained solely on video and one trained with the joint objective. A simple plot showing training loss or FVD versus wall-clock time or training steps would clarify the efficiency gains. Furthermore, the paper should address whether this joint training introduces a bias in the distribution of motion dynamics compared to training on video-only data, as this is a potential trade-off for the observed stability.

- Missing sensitivity analysis for reconstruction guidance hyperparameter: Equation 7 introduces a reconstruction guidance weight w_r, which is central to the proposed sampling technique for long-sequence generation. The paper presents results for specific values of w_r but does not characterize the sensitivity of the model to this parameter. A systematic study showing how FVD and qualitative temporal coherence vary across a range of w_r (e.g., 0.1 to 10.0) is necessary to determine if the method requires careful tuning for different datasets or if it is robust. Without this, it is difficult for practitioners to implement the method successfully on new tasks.

Recommendation: minor revision. The paper presents a valuable and well-executed method, but it requires more rigorous validation of its temporal claims and a clearer understanding of the proposed sampling hyperparameters. Addressing the evaluation gaps and providing a more detailed analysis of the architectural components will significantly improve the paper's impact.

Revision targets:

- Include a human evaluation or a more robust metric to specifically assess temporal consistency and flickering.

- Add an ablation study comparing the factorized space-time attention architecture against a non-factorized baseline.

- Provide a sensitivity analysis for the reconstruction guidance weight w_r to demonstrate its robustness.

- Include a brief discussion or analysis of the computational scaling and memory requirements for long-duration video synthesis.

comments.title: Inconsistency in Langevin correction for conditional sampling
comments.quote:
Our version of this sampler alternates between the ancestral sampler step (4) and a Langevin correction step of the form

$\mathbf{z}_{s}\leftarrow\mathbf{z}_{s}-\frac{1}{2}\delta\sigma_{s}\bm{\epsilon}_{\theta}(\mathbf{z}_{s})+\sqrt{\delta}\sigma_{s}\bm{\epsilon}^{\prime}$ (5)
comments.explanation: The Langevin step (5) uses the unconditional score, which is inappropriate for conditional generation. If the goal is to sample from $p(\mathbf{z}_s|\mathbf{c})$, the Langevin dynamics must target the conditional distribution. Using the unconditional score drives samples toward the unconditional marginal, effectively counteracting the conditioning signal. Please update the equation to include the conditioning signal $\mathbf{c}$ and ensure the dynamics correctly sample from the conditional distribution.

comments.title: Missing guidance adjustment in Langevin correction
comments.quote:
This method samples using adjusted model predictions $\tilde{\bm{\epsilon}}_{\theta}$, constructed via

$\tilde{\bm{\epsilon}}_{\theta}(\mathbf{z}_{t},\mathbf{c})=(1+w)\bm{\epsilon}_{\theta}(\mathbf{z}_{t},\mathbf{c})-w\bm{\epsilon}_{\theta}(\mathbf{z}_{t}),$ (6)
comments.explanation: Even if the Langevin step is updated to use the conditional score, it remains inconsistent with the classifier-free guidance framework. If the Langevin step is intended to refine samples in a conditional task, it must utilize the guidance-adjusted score $\tilde{\bm{\epsilon}}_{\theta}$ to maintain consistency with the ancestral sampling steps. Without this, the Langevin step performs correction against the unguided conditional distribution, which likely degrades the quality gains from classifier-free guidance. Please update the Langevin step to use the guidance-adjusted score.

comments.title: Inconsistent FID values in Table 1
comments.explanation: The FID values reported in Table 1 for existing methods (e.g., 26998 for MoCoGAN) are orders of magnitude higher than standard FID results for UCF101, which typically fall between 10 and 100. It appears the authors may have reported the squared Fréchet distance or a non-normalized metric, making the comparison to literature misleading. Please verify the metric used and ensure the table entries reflect standard evaluation practices.

comments.title: Inaccurate gradient variable in Equation 8
comments.quote: $\hat{\mathbf{x}}_{\theta}(\mathbf{z}_{t})=\hat{\mathbf{x}}_{\theta}(\mathbf{z}_{t})-\frac{w_{r}\alpha_{t}}{2}\nabla_{\mathbf{z}_{s}}\lVert\mathbf{x}^{a}-\hat{\mathbf{x}}_{\theta}^{a}(\mathbf{z}_{t})\rVert_{2}^{2}$ (8)
comments.explanation: The gradient in Equation (8) is taken with respect to $\mathbf{z}_s$, which is inconsistent with the denoising objective that depends on the current noisy latent $\mathbf{z}_t$. In diffusion sampling, the guidance term must be computed with respect to the variable being denoised at the current step $t$. Please correct the gradient to be with respect to $\mathbf{z}_t$.

comments.title: Mischaracterization of joint training benefits
comments.quote: Adding independent image frames has the effect of reducing variance of the gradient at the expense of some bias for the video modeling objective, and thus it can be seen as a memory optimization to fit more independent examples in a batch.
comments.explanation: The authors frame joint training primarily as a variance reduction technique. However, the empirical results in Table 4 show a massive improvement in FVD (from ~203 to ~59) and FID-avg (from ~37 to ~15). This magnitude of improvement suggests that joint training acts as a powerful regularizer or multi-task learning mechanism that improves spatial feature representation, rather than just a memory optimization. Please revise the text to acknowledge these auxiliary spatial learning benefits.

comments.title: Inconsistent FVD trends with guidance weight
comments.quote: As expected, there is clear improvement in the Inception Score-like metrics with higher guidance weight, while the FID-like metrics improve and then degrade with increasing guidance weight.
comments.explanation: The text claims that FID-like metrics improve and then degrade with increasing guidance weight. However, Table 5 shows that FVD values for Frameskip 1 monotonically degrade as the guidance weight increases from 1.0 to 5.0 (41.65 to 163.74). The claim of an initial improvement phase is not supported by the data. Please revise the text to accurately reflect the observed performance trend.

comments.title: Undefined notation in reconstruction guidance
comments.quote: where  $\hat{\mathbf{x}}_g^a (\mathbf{z}_t)$  is our model's reconstruction of the low-resolution video from  $\mathbf{z}_t$
comments.explanation: The notation $\hat{\mathbf{x}}_g^a (\mathbf{z}_t)$ is introduced to explain the reconstruction process, but the subscript $g$ is never defined, and the context implies it is intended to be the same operator as $\hat{\mathbf{x}}_{\theta}^a (\mathbf{z}_t)$ used in Equation (8). Please replace $\hat{\mathbf{x}}_g^a$ with $\hat{\mathbf{x}}_{\theta}^a$ to maintain consistency.

coarse__deepseek-v4-flash.label: Coarse (deepseek-v4-flash)
coarse__deepseek-v4-flash.overall_feedback:
This paper introduces Video Diffusion Models (VDM), extending image diffusion models to video generation with a 3D U-Net architecture, joint image-video training, and a novel reconstruction-guided sampling method for conditional generation. The paper demonstrates strong quantitative results on unconditional video generation, video prediction, and text-conditioned video generation benchmarks. While the work is foundational and influential, several methodological concerns and framing issues warrant attention.

The paper makes a significant contribution by being one of the first to successfully apply diffusion models to video generation, demonstrating state-of-the-art results on multiple benchmarks. The proposed architecture and training techniques are well-motivated and show clear empirical benefits. However, the evaluation of text-conditioned generation lacks rigorous alignment metrics, the reconstruction guidance method's theoretical justification is incomplete, and some experimental details are insufficient for reproducibility.

Overview issues:

- Incomplete evaluation of text-conditioned video generation: The paper claims 'first results on a large text-conditioned video generation task' but evaluates these results only with FVD, FID, and IS metrics. These metrics measure visual quality and diversity but do not assess text-video alignment. For a text-conditioned model, it is essential to report metrics like CLIP score (measuring semantic alignment between text and generated frames) or human evaluation (e.g., preference judgments). Without such evaluation, the claim of meaningful text-conditioned generation is unsupported. The paper should include CLIP-based alignment scores and/or a human evaluation study to validate that generated videos actually correspond to the given text prompts.

- Theoretical justification for reconstruction guidance is incomplete: Section 3.1 derives reconstruction guidance by approximating q(x^a | z_t) with a Gaussian centered at the model's reconstruction. The derivation claims this approximation becomes exact as t→0, but the sampling process involves many steps at various t values, and the approximation error at larger t is not analyzed. The paper does not discuss how the approximation quality degrades for larger noise levels or how the weighting factor w_r should be chosen to compensate. A more rigorous analysis or empirical study of the approximation error across different t values would strengthen the method's credibility. Additionally, the connection between the gradient term and classifier guidance is mentioned but not formally justified.

- Joint training on images and videos may introduce domain mismatch: The paper reports that adding independent image frames (from the same video dataset) reduces gradient variance and improves metrics. However, the images are drawn from the same video dataset, not from a separate large-scale image dataset. The paper acknowledges this limitation but does not explore the effect of using out-of-domain images (e.g., from ImageNet). If the image distribution differs significantly from the video frames, the joint training could introduce bias that degrades video-specific temporal coherence. An ablation study using images from a different domain would clarify whether the benefit is due to variance reduction or domain-specific augmentation.

- Autoregressive extension may suffer from error accumulation: The paper demonstrates autoregressive extension from 16 frames to 64 frames using reconstruction guidance. While the results show improvement over the replacement method, the evaluation only extends to 64 frames. For longer sequences (hundreds of frames), error accumulation could cause drift or loss of temporal coherence. The paper does not discuss the stability of the method for longer extensions or provide quantitative metrics for longer sequences. A study of FVD as a function of sequence length (e.g., 16, 32, 64, 128 frames) would help characterize the method's limitations. Additionally, the paper should discuss potential failure modes such as mode collapse or repetitive motion patterns in long generations.

- Lack of ablation on the number of temporal attention blocks: The architecture uses factorized space-time attention with temporal attention blocks after each spatial attention block. The paper does not ablate the placement or number of temporal attention blocks. It is possible that fewer temporal attention blocks (e.g., only at certain resolutions) could reduce computational cost without sacrificing quality, or that more blocks could improve temporal coherence. Given that the paper claims computational efficiency as a motivation for factorized attention, an ablation study on the temporal attention configuration would strengthen the architectural justification.

- Evaluation protocol inconsistencies in video prediction benchmarks: In Section 4.2, the paper notes that prior works (DVD-GAN, TriVD-GAN-FP) used different evaluation protocols (sampling with replacement, different random seeds) for Kinetics-600. The paper reports that using the alternative protocol raises its FVD from 16.2 to 16.9. While this is transparent, it raises questions about the fairness of comparisons with methods evaluated under different protocols. The paper should either re-evaluate prior methods under the same protocol or provide a more detailed analysis of how protocol differences affect rankings. Additionally, the paper does not report confidence intervals for all metrics (e.g., Table 2 lacks error bars), making it difficult to assess statistical significance of improvements.

- Missing text-video alignment evaluation: The paper claims 'first results on a large text-conditioned video generation task' but evaluates these results only with FVD, FID, and IS metrics. These metrics measure visual quality and diversity but do not assess text-video alignment. For a text-conditioned model, it is essential to report metrics like CLIP score (measuring semantic alignment between text and generated frames) or human evaluation (e.g., preference judgments). Without such evaluation, the claim of meaningful text-conditioned generation is unsupported. The paper should include CLIP-based alignment scores and/or a human evaluation study to validate that generated videos actually correspond to the given text prompts.

- No analysis of approximation error in reconstruction guidance: Section 3.1 derives reconstruction guidance by approximating q(x^a | z_t) with a Gaussian centered at the model's reconstruction. The derivation claims this approximation becomes exact as t→0, but the sampling process involves many steps at various t values, and the approximation error at larger t is not analyzed. The paper does not discuss how the approximation quality degrades for larger noise levels or how the weighting factor w_r should be chosen to compensate. A more rigorous analysis or empirical study of the approximation error across different t values would strengthen the method's credibility. Additionally, the connection between the gradient term and classifier guidance is mentioned but not formally justified.

- Autoregressive extension evaluation limited to 64 frames: The paper demonstrates autoregressive extension from 16 frames to 64 frames using reconstruction guidance. While the results show improvement over the replacement method, the evaluation only extends to 64 frames. For longer sequences (hundreds of frames), error accumulation could cause drift or loss of temporal coherence. The paper does not discuss the stability of the method for longer extensions or provide quantitative metrics for longer sequences. A study of FVD as a function of sequence length (e.g., 16, 32, 64, 128 frames) would help characterize the method's limitations. Additionally, the paper should discuss potential failure modes such as mode collapse or repetitive motion patterns in long generations.

Recommendation: major revision

Revision targets:

- Add text-video alignment evaluation (CLIP score or human evaluation) for text-conditioned generation results.

- Provide a more rigorous analysis of the reconstruction guidance approximation error, including empirical study across different noise levels and t values.

- Include an ablation study on the effect of using out-of-domain images for joint training, and discuss potential domain mismatch issues.

- Evaluate autoregressive extension for longer sequences (e.g., 128 frames) and report FVD as a function of sequence length to characterize error accumulation.

- Add an ablation study on the number and placement of temporal attention blocks to justify the architectural choices.

- Report confidence intervals for all quantitative results and clarify evaluation protocols for fair comparison with prior work.

comments.title: Reconstruction guidance derivation assumes Gaussian approximation without justification
comments.quote: Since $q(\mathbf{x}^{a}|\mathbf{z}_{t})$ is not available in closed form, however, we instead propose to approximate it using a Gaussian of the form $q(\mathbf{x}^{a}|\mathbf{z}_{t})\approx\mathcal{N}[\hat{\mathbf{x}}_{\theta}^{a}(\mathbf{z}_{t}),(\sigma_{t}^{2}/\alpha_{t}^{2})\mathbf{l}]$
comments.explanation: The paper claims this approximation becomes exact as t → 0 and is good for larger t, but provides no justification or reference for why the conditional distribution q(x^a | z_t) should be approximately Gaussian with covariance (σ_t^2 / α_t^2) I. In general, q(x^a | z_t) is not Gaussian because z_t is a noisy version of the full video x = [x^a, x^b], and the marginal over x^b is integrated out. The approximation would require that the posterior p(x^a | z_t) is well-approximated by a Gaussian centered at the MMSE estimate, which is not generally true for non-Gaussian data distributions. The authors should either provide a theoretical justification (e.g., using the Tweedie formula and a Laplace approximation) or cite prior work that validates this approximation in similar settings.

comments.title: Inconsistent evaluation protocol for Kinetics-600
comments.quote: In our reported results we sample test videos without replacement, and we use the same randomly selected subsequences for generating model samples and for defining the ground truth, since this results in the lowest bias and variance in the reported FVD metric. However, from personal communication we learned that [33, 14] instead sampled with replacement, and used a different random seed when sampling the ground truth data. We find that this way of evaluating raises the FVD obtained by our model slightly, from 16.2 to 16.9.
comments.explanation: The paper acknowledges that prior works used a different evaluation protocol (sampling with replacement, different random seed) and that this raises the FVD from 16.2 to 16.9. However, the paper does not re-evaluate prior methods under the same protocol. This makes the comparison in Table 3 potentially unfair: the reported FVD of 16.2 for Video Diffusion may not be directly comparable to the FVD values of prior methods (e.g., TrIVD-GAN-FP at 25.74) if those were computed under the alternative protocol. The paper should either re-evaluate prior methods under the same protocol or explicitly state that the comparison is approximate. Add a note in Table 3 or the text clarifying that the FVD values for prior methods may not be directly comparable due to protocol differences.

comments.title: Missing Inception Score for several methods in Table 3
comments.quote:
Table 3: Video prediction on Kinetics-600.

|  Method | FVD↓ | IS↑  |
| --- | --- | --- |
|  Video Transformer [59] | 170 ± 5 | - |
|  DVD-GAN-FP [14] | 69.1 ± 0.78 | - |
|  Video VQ-VAE [57] | 64.3 ± 2.04 | - |
|  CCVS [31] | 55 ± 1 | - |
|  TrIVD-GAN-FP [33] | 25.74 ± 0.66 | 12.54  |
|  Transframer [35] | 25.4 | - |
|  Video Diffusion (ours) | - | - |
|  ancestral, 256 steps | 18.6 | 15.39  |
|  Langevin, 128 steps | 16.2 ± 0.34 | 15.64  |
comments.explanation: The table reports Inception Score (IS) only for TrIVD-GAN-FP and Video Diffusion, but not for other methods. The text states 'Like previous works, we calculate FVD and Inception Score using the I3D network', implying IS is a standard metric for this benchmark. The absence of IS for most prior methods makes it difficult to compare the IS improvements claimed by the authors. The paper should either provide IS values for all prior methods (by re-evaluating them or citing existing values) or explain why IS is omitted for those methods. Add IS values for all methods in Table 3, or add a footnote explaining that IS was not reported in the original papers.

comments.title: Gradient variance claim lacks justification
comments.quote: Adding independent image frames has the effect of reducing variance of the gradient at the expense of some bias for the video modeling objective, and thus it can be seen as a memory optimization to fit more independent examples in a batch.
comments.explanation: The paper asserts that adding independent image frames reduces gradient variance and introduces bias, but provides no derivation or empirical evidence. This is a significant claim about optimization dynamics that requires support. The authors should either provide a theoretical analysis (e.g., showing how the gradient variance decomposes with added image frames) or present empirical measurements of gradient variance during training with different numbers of image frames. Without this, the statement remains an unsupported assertion. Rewrite the quoted text to include a brief justification or reference to future work, or remove the claim if it cannot be substantiated.

comments.title: Table 4 dual values unexplained
comments.explanation: Each cell contains two values separated by a slash, but the paper does not explain what these represent (e.g., different runs, random seeds, or evaluation protocols). This ambiguity prevents readers from interpreting the results. The authors should add a footnote or caption explaining the meaning of the two values, or restructure the table to clarify (e.g., separate columns for each run).

comments.title: Masking mechanism for joint training unclear
comments.quote: we concatenate random independent image frames to the end of each video sampled from the dataset, and we mask the attention in the temporal attention blocks to prevent mixing information across video frames and each individual image frame.
comments.explanation: The description of the masking mechanism is vague. It is not specified how the attention mask is constructed: does it prevent all cross-attention between video frames and image frames, or only between different image frames? Also, does the mask allow each image frame to attend to itself? Without precise details, the implementation is ambiguous and cannot be reproduced. The authors should provide a formal definition of the attention mask (e.g., a binary matrix) or cite a reference that specifies the exact masking strategy.

comments.title: Table 5 lacks error bars or variance estimates
comments.quote: Table 5 reports results that verify the effectiveness of classifier-free guidance [20] on text-to-video generation. As expected, there is clear improvement in the Inception Score-like metrics with higher guidance weight, while the FID-like metrics improve and then degrade with increasing guidance weight.
comments.explanation: The table reports only point estimates (e.g., FVD 41.65/43.70) without any standard deviation, confidence interval, or indication of how many runs were averaged. Without variance information, the reader cannot assess whether the observed improvements (e.g., FID-avg dropping from 12.49 to 10.53 when guidance weight increases from 1.0 to 2.0) are statistically significant or merely noise. The paper's own evaluation standards for this field state that 'ablation studies must isolate the contributions ... with statistical significance reported.' Add standard deviations or confidence intervals to each metric entry, or at minimum state the number of independent runs used to compute each value.

comments.title: FVD degrades sharply at guidance weight 5.0 but no explanation
comments.explanation: The FVD at guidance weight 5.0 (163.74/160.21) is roughly 3–4× worse than at weight 2.0 (50.19/48.79) for frameskip 1, and similarly for frameskip 4. This dramatic degradation is not discussed in the text. The paper states only that 'FID-like metrics improve and then degrade with increasing guidance weight,' but the magnitude of FVD degradation far exceeds the modest FID-avg increase from 10.53 to 13.54. Since FVD is the primary video quality metric, this collapse warrants explanation. The text should address why FVD degrades so severely at high guidance weights while IS continues to improve, and whether this indicates a trade-off between per-frame quality and temporal coherence.

comments.title: Claim of 'clear improvement' is contradicted by FVD at weight 5.0
comments.quote: Table 5 reports results that verify the effectiveness of classifier-free guidance [20] on text-to-video generation. As expected, there is clear improvement in the Inception Score-like metrics with higher guidance weight, while the FID-like metrics improve and then degrade with increasing guidance weight.
comments.explanation: The statement 'clear improvement in the Inception Score-like metrics with higher guidance weight' is accurate for IS-avg and IS-first, which monotonically increase from weight 1.0 to 5.0. However, the FVD — the primary metric for video generation quality — degrades at weight 5.0 to values (163.74, 160.21) that are far worse than the unguided baseline (41.65, 43.70). Claiming 'effectiveness' of classifier-free guidance based solely on IS improvements while ignoring the catastrophic FVD collapse at high guidance is misleading. The paper should either qualify the claim by noting that guidance improves IS but harms FVD at high weights, or explain why FVD is less relevant for this evaluation.

comments.title: Overclaim regarding reconstruction guidance outperforming existing methods
comments.quote: We have additionally demonstrated the benefits of joint image-video training and classifier-free guidance for video diffusion models on both video and image sample quality metrics, and we also introduced a new reconstruction-guided conditional sampling method that outperforms existing replacement or imputation methods for conditional sampling from unconditionally trained models.
comments.explanation: The conclusion claims that reconstruction guidance 'outperforms existing replacement or imputation methods.' However, the paper's evaluation (Section 4.3.3) only compares reconstruction guidance against a single baseline (the 'replacement method') on the specific task of autoregressive video extension. The claim of outperforming 'existing replacement or imputation methods' is broader than what was demonstrated — the paper does not compare against other imputation approaches (e.g., the method of Saharia et al. for image inpainting, or any video-specific imputation baseline). The claim should be narrowed to reflect the actual comparison performed. Rewrite the quoted text as '...we also introduced a new reconstruction-guided conditional sampling method that outperforms the replacement method for autoregressive video extension from unconditionally trained models.'

comments.title: Unsupported claim about generating long sequences via frame interpolation
comments.quote: Our reconstruction guidance method can generate long sequences using either frame interpolation (or temporal super-resolution) or extrapolation in an auto-regressive fashion, and also can perform spatial super-resolution.
comments.explanation: The conclusion states that reconstruction guidance can generate long sequences via frame interpolation (temporal super-resolution). However, the paper's experiments (Section 4.3.3) only demonstrate autoregressive extrapolation (generating future frames from past frames) and spatial super-resolution. Temporal super-resolution (interpolating intermediate frames between given frames) is mentioned in Section 3.1 as a potential application but is never evaluated or demonstrated with results. The claim that the method 'can generate long sequences using either frame interpolation...or extrapolation' is not supported by experimental evidence. Remove 'frame interpolation (or temporal super-resolution)' from the quoted sentence, or add a reference to a demonstration or experiment that validates this capability.

comments.title: Mischaracterization of concurrent work
comments.quote: Concurrent work [63] proposes a diffusion-based approach to video generation that uses an image diffusion model to predict each individual frame within a RNN temporal autoregressive model. Our video diffusion model, by contrast, jointly models entire videos (blocks of frames) using a 3D video architecture with interleaved spatial and temporal attention, and we extend to long sequence lengths by filling in frames or autoregressive temporal extension.
comments.explanation: The description of concurrent work [63] (which is likely 'Diffusion Models for Video Prediction and Infilling' by Ho et al., 2022) is inaccurate. That work does not use an RNN temporal autoregressive model; instead, it conditions a diffusion model on masked frames via a conditioning mask and generates all frames in parallel, not sequentially with an RNN. The paper under review itself cites [63] as a diffusion-based video prediction method, but the characterization here misrepresents its architecture. This could mislead readers about the novelty of the proposed joint modeling approach.

comments.title: Mischaracterization of joint training benefits as demonstrated for both metrics
comments.quote: We have additionally demonstrated the benefits of joint image-video training and classifier-free guidance for video diffusion models on both video and image sample quality metrics
comments.explanation: The conclusion claims that joint image-video training benefits 'both video and image sample quality metrics.' However, the ablation study in Section 4.3.1 only evaluates video metrics (FVD) and image metrics (FID) for the joint training variant. The paper does not report whether the improvement in image metrics is statistically significant or whether it holds across different model sizes. More importantly, the claim implies that joint training improves both types of metrics simultaneously, but the paper does not discuss potential trade-offs (e.g., improved image quality at the expense of temporal coherence). The claim should be qualified to reflect the specific metrics and settings where improvement was observed. Rewrite as 'We have additionally demonstrated the benefits of joint image-video training and classifier-free guidance for video diffusion models on video sample quality metrics (FVD) and image sample quality metrics (FID) in our ablation study.'
