coarse__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.label: Coarse (qwen3.6-35b-a3b)
coarse__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.overall_feedback:
The paper presents a straightforward but effective extension of image diffusion models to the video domain, introducing a factorized 3D U-Net architecture and a reconstruction-guided sampling strategy for temporal and spatial extension. The experimental results on UCF101, BAIR, and Kinetics-600 are strong, and the joint image-video training strategy offers a practical engineering solution for memory-constrained training. However, the manuscript contains notation errors in its core sampling equations, contradicts itself regarding the statistical benefits of mixed-data training, and lacks the compute and alignment metrics expected for top-tier generative modeling venues.

This work delivers a clean architectural adaptation of diffusion models for video, backed by competitive benchmark scores and a practical conditional sampling technique. The factorized space-time attention design and reconstruction guidance are well-motivated and empirically validated. The paper's main weakness lies in incomplete reporting of computational budgets, inconsistent framing of its training methodology, and an overreliance on standard perceptual metrics that fail to capture temporal fidelity or text-video alignment.

Overview issues:

- Self-referential and dimensionally inconsistent guidance equations: Equations (7) and (8) in Section 3.1 define the guided model prediction using the exact same symbol on both sides of the equality, rendering the update rule impossible to implement as written. Equation (8) further differentiates with respect to $\mathbf{z}_s$ while evaluating all model terms at $\mathbf{z}_t$, which breaks dimensional consistency. Readers attempting to reproduce the reconstruction-guided sampling method will hit a hard wall without guessing the intended notation. This notation error obscures a central methodological contribution and must be corrected before publication. The authors should introduce a distinct symbol for the guided prediction (e.g., $\hat{\mathbf{x}}_{\theta}^{b,\text{guided}}$) and align the gradient variable with the diffusion timestep $\mathbf{z}_t$.

- Contradictory framing of joint image-video training benefits: The abstract claims that jointly training on image and video data reduces the variance of minibatch gradients and speeds up optimization. Section 4.3.1 later reframes this exact technique as a memory optimization that allows fitting more independent examples per batch, explicitly noting it introduces bias to the video objective. These statements conflict. If the gradient variance drops because the effective batch size increases, the speedup is a hardware utilization artifact rather than a statistical property of the mixed loss. This ambiguity weakens the theoretical justification for the training protocol. The authors should clarify whether the variance reduction is a direct consequence of the joint objective or a byproduct of increased batch throughput, and report the exact bias-variance trade-off curve for the video modeling loss.

- Missing compute and inference step budgets for state-of-the-art claims: Tables 1 through 3 report state-of-the-art FVD and FID scores but provide no information on training FLOPs, inference sampling steps, or memory footprint relative to the baselines. Diffusion models are inherently iterative, and sample quality scales heavily with the number of denoising steps. A model achieving lower FVD using 512 sampling steps while baselines use 50 steps does not represent a fair efficiency comparison. The evaluation standards for this subfield require matched compute or step budgets to validate SOTA claims. The authors must add a comprehensive table reporting training compute, inference steps, and memory usage for all competing methods, and re-evaluate their results under identical step budgets to ensure fairness.

- Overreliance on FVD without temporal coherence or human evaluation: The paper evaluates video generation almost exclusively using FVD, FID, and IS. FVD relies on an I3D network that captures short-term motion but is brittle and correlates poorly with human perception of long-range temporal consistency. The qualitative samples in Figures 2 through 4 show promise, but the manuscript offers no systematic metric for temporal smoothness, physical plausibility, or object permanence across extended sequences. Given that temporal coherence is the core challenge this work addresses, relying solely on FVD leaves a critical evaluation gap. The authors should supplement FVD with jitter-penalty metrics or Fréchet Video Distance variants that penalize frame-to-frame inconsistency, and include a human evaluation study focused on motion realism.

- Unverified text-video semantic alignment metrics: Section 4.3 highlights first results on a large text-conditioned video generation task but reports only FID and IS metrics. These statistics measure pixel-level and feature-level distributions, not the semantic match between the prompt and the generated content. High FID/IS scores do not guarantee that the video actually depicts the described actions or objects, especially for complex scenes. Without alignment metrics, the claim of progress on text-conditioned generation remains unsupported. The authors should compute CLIP-I and CLIP-V scores, and report a human preference study or automated win-rate comparison against human captions to substantiate the text-conditioning results.

- Inference cost of reconstruction guidance is unanalyzed: The reconstruction-guided sampling method requires backpropagating through the denoising network at every iterative step to compute the gradient in Equation 7. The paper never discusses the computational overhead this introduces. Readers will wonder how the memory footprint and latency scale during sampling compared to standard ancestral or predictor-corrector methods. This gap matters because iterative guidance that demands full backward passes can easily double or triple inference time, undermining the practical utility of the proposed extension technique. The authors should report the per-step FLOPs added by the gradient term, measure the actual wall-clock sampling speed relative to baselines, and discuss whether gradient checkpointing or approximations are necessary for high-resolution video generation.

- Image diffusion special case lacks empirical verification: The authors claim the 3D space-time architecture is a straightforward extension of 2D U-Nets that preserves spatial priors while enabling temporal modeling. To substantiate this, the paper should compute image generation metrics for the masked 3D model on a standard dataset like CIFAR-10 or a subset of ImageNet. Comparing the masked model's FID and IS against a standard 2D U-Net trained under identical conditions would verify that the architectural changes do not inadvertently weaken the spatial generative capacity. Without this baseline check, the claim that the extension is natural and does not degrade image quality remains unverified.

- Autoregressive error accumulation is unquantified: The reconstruction guidance method enables autoregressive extension of short clips into longer sequences, but the paper provides no analysis of how generation quality degrades as the sequence length increases. Table 6 only reports metrics for extending 16 frames to 64 frames. Readers will wonder whether the temporal coherence holds for longer horizons or if errors compound across autoregressive blocks. The authors should track a coherence metric, such as frame-to-frame correlation or sliding-window FVD, as a function of total generated frames. This would clarify the practical limits of the extension technique and guide future work on memory or recurrence mechanisms for long-form video.

Recommendation: Major revision. The paper presents a solid architectural extension of diffusion models to video, but the self-referential equations in Section 3.1 and the contradictory explanation of joint training motivation require immediate correction. Furthermore, the absence of compute budgets, inference step counts, and text-video alignment metrics prevents a fair assessment of the claimed state-of-the-art performance and limits the paper's impact at a top venue.

Revision targets:

- Correct Equations (7) and (8) in Section 3.1 to use distinct notation for guided predictions and align gradient variables with the diffusion timestep.

- Clarify the statistical vs. engineering benefits of joint image-video training in the abstract and Section 4.3.1, and report the bias-variance trade-off for the video objective.

- Add a compute and inference budget table reporting training FLOPs, sampling steps, and memory usage for all baselines and the proposed method.

- Introduce temporal coherence metrics (e.g., jitter penalties or human evaluation) to supplement FVD and validate long-range motion consistency.

- Report CLIP-I/CLIP-V scores and human win-rates to substantiate the text-conditioned generation claims.

comments.title: Incorrect feature extractor cited for FID and IS
comments.quote: imilar to previous methods, we use the C3D network  $[51]^2$  for calculating FID and IS, using 10,000 samples generated from our model.
comments.explanation: FID and IS are standard metrics computed using features from the Inception-V3 network, not C3D. C3D is a 3D convolutional network typically used for action recognition, and it is not the standard backbone for calculating FID or IS. For video generation, the standard metric is FVD, which relies on I3D features. Citing C3D for FID and IS misstates the evaluation protocol and will prevent readers from reproducing the reported scores. The authors should verify which network was actually used to extract features for these metrics and correct the notation accordingly.

comments.title: Invalid justification for cross-resolution metric comparison
comments.quote: odel. C3D internally resizes input data to the  $112\times 112$  spatial resolution, so perceptual scores are approximately comparable even when the data is sampled at a different resolution originally. A
comments.explanation: Resizing input data to a fixed resolution for feature extraction does not justify comparing FID/IS scores across models trained at different native resolutions. FID measures the distance between the feature distributions of real and generated data; if the underlying data distribution changes with resolution (e.g., 64x64 vs 128x128), the feature distributions will shift regardless of the extractor's internal preprocessing. Consequently, a 64x64 model's FID cannot be fairly compared to a 128x128 model's FID simply because the feature extractor resizes inputs. This logical gap weakens the claim that the 64x64 model achieves the best metrics against higher-resolution baselines. The authors should restrict comparisons to models evaluated at identical resolutions or explicitly acknowledge that cross-resolution metric comparisons are invalid.

comments.title: Contradictory framing of joint image-video training benefits
comments.quote: Adding independent image frames has the effect of reducing variance of the gradient at the expense of some bias for the video modeling objective, and thus it can be seen as a memory optimization to fit more independent examples in a batch.
comments.explanation: The abstract claims that jointly training on image and video data reduces the variance of minibatch gradients and speeds up optimization. Section 4.3.1 later reframes this exact technique as a memory optimization that allows fitting more independent examples per batch, explicitly noting it introduces bias to the video objective. These statements conflict. If the gradient variance drops because the effective batch size increases, the speedup is a hardware utilization artifact rather than a statistical property of the mixed loss. This ambiguity weakens the theoretical justification for the training protocol. The authors should clarify whether the variance reduction is a direct consequence of the joint objective or a byproduct of increased batch throughput, and report the exact bias-variance trade-off curve for the video modeling loss.

comments.title: Mischaracterization of methodological contribution
comments.quote: We show that high quality videos can be generated using essentially the standard formulation of the Gaussian diffusion model [46], with little modification other than straightforward architectural changes to accommodate video data within the memory constraints of deep learning accelerators.
comments.explanation: This sentence claims the work relies on little modification other than straightforward architectural changes, which directly contradicts the paper's central contribution: a novel reconstruction guidance method for autoregressive sampling. The technical sections detail a sampling procedure that requires iterative backpropagation through the denoising network to compute guidance gradients, a significant algorithmic departure from standard diffusion sampling. Framing the contribution solely as architectural adjustments misleads readers about the actual novelty and complexity of the proposed method. The authors should revise this claim to explicitly acknowledge the novel guidance and sampling methodology as a core contribution alongside the architectural changes.

comments.title: Ambiguous attention masking mechanism
comments.quote: To implement this joint training, we concatenate random independent image frames to the end of each video sampled from the dataset, and we mask the attention in the temporal attention blocks to prevent mixing information across video frames and each individual image frame.
comments.explanation: The phrasing implies that temporal attention is masked across the entire sequence, which would prevent video frames from attending to one another and effectively remove the model's capacity to learn temporal dynamics. The intended design almost certainly masks only the cross-attention between the video sequence tokens and the concatenated image tokens, while preserving intra-video temporal attention. Without this clarification, readers cannot understand how the model maintains temporal coherence during generation. Rewrite the passage to explicitly state that the mask blocks cross-attention between video and image tokens, preserving temporal dependencies within the video sequence.

comments.title: Unsubstantiated statistical claim regarding conditioning frames
comments.quote: In our reported results we sample test videos without replacement, and we use the same randomly selected subsequences for generating model samples and for defining the ground truth, since this results in the lowest bias and variance in the reported FVD metric.
comments.explanation: Fixing the conditioning subsequences for both the model and ground truth evaluates the conditional generation distribution, not the unconditional FVD. While this choice reduces the variance of the FVD estimator by controlling for input variability, it does not minimize bias; in fact, it may increase bias if the model's conditional distribution diverges from the true data distribution. The authors should clarify that they are reporting conditional FVD and explicitly state that fixing the subsequences is done to reduce estimator variance, not to minimize metric bias. Adding a brief justification or citation for this evaluation choice would strengthen the methodological rigor.

comments.title: Ambiguous Inception Score calculation protocol
comments.quote: For IS, we report two numbers which are averaged scores across 1 split and 10 splits of samples, respectively.
comments.explanation: The phrase averaged scores across 1 split and 10 splits is logically inconsistent. Inception Score requires a fixed dataset split for evaluation, or an average over multiple random splits. Clarify whether the first reported value is computed on a single fixed split and the second is the mean across 10 independent splits. Rewrite the sentence to explicitly state the evaluation protocol for each metric, for example: We report IS computed on a single fixed split and averaged over 10 random splits, respectively. This prevents confusion about how the two numbers are derived.

comments.title: Undefined slash notation in Table 5 values
comments.quote:
free guidance.

Table 5: Effect of classifier-free guidance on text-to-video generation (large models). Sample quality is reported for  $16 \times 64 \times 64$  models trained on frameskip 1 and 4 data. The model was jointly trained on 8 independent image frames per 16-fr
comments.explanation: The table cells consistently present pairs of numbers separated by a slash (e.g., 41.65/43.70 for FVD at frameskip 1, guidance 1.0). The caption and surrounding text do not define what these two values represent. Without this clarification, readers cannot determine whether the numbers indicate mean and standard deviation across multiple seeds, results from two distinct model runs, or another statistical summary. This omission obscures the reliability of the reported metrics. Rewrite the table caption to include Values are reported as mean/std across two independent random seeds. because the slash-separated pairs are currently undefined and prevent accurate interpretation of metric reliability.

comments.title: Missing guidance weight in Figure 3 caption
comments.quote:
re 3: Example frames from a random selection of videos generated by our  $16 \times 64 \times 64$  text-conditioned model. Left: unguided samples, right: guided samples using classifier-free guidance.

Ta
comments.explanation: The figure caption states that the right column shows guided samples but omits the specific guidance weight used. Since sample quality and fidelity vary significantly with the guidance weight, as demonstrated in Table 5, readers cannot know which setting produced these visuals. This omission makes it difficult to assess whether the qualitative results correspond to the optimal weight or a higher, potentially degraded setting. Rewrite the figure caption to state ...right: guided samples using classifier-free guidance with weight 2.0. because the qualitative results depend heavily on this hyperparameter and the current description leaves the setting ambiguous.

comments.title: Ambiguous dual values in Table 6 metrics
comments.quote: |  Guidance weight | Conditioning method | FVD↓ | FID-avg↓ | IS-avg↑ | FID-first↓ | IS-first↑  |
comments.explanation: Every metric column in Table 6 contains two numbers separated by a slash. The manuscript never explains what these paired values represent. They likely correspond to two independent random seeds, evaluation splits, or model variants, but without a definition, readers cannot assess the reported variance or replicate the setup. Please add a clear note in the table caption or footnote specifying the meaning of the slash notation and the number of runs or seeds used. Rewrite the table caption to include: Values are reported as [Run 1]/[Run 2] (or [Seed A]/[Seed B]) to indicate performance across independent trials.

comments.title: Misclassification of Inception Score as perceptual
comments.quote: In Table 6 we present results on generating longer videos using both techniques, and find that our proposed method indeed improves over the replacement method in terms of perceptual quality scores.
comments.explanation: The authors describe the reported metrics as perceptual quality scores, but the table includes FVD, FID, and Inception Score (IS). FVD and FID evaluate perceptual fidelity and distribution matching, whereas IS measures image quality and diversity based on classifier confidence. Grouping IS under perceptual metrics is technically inaccurate and may mislead readers about what aspect of generation quality is being measured. Change the phrase to generation quality and diversity metrics or explicitly separate perceptual metrics from diversity metrics in the text. Rewrite the sentence to: ...find that our proposed method indeed improves over the replacement method in terms of generation quality and diversity metrics.

comments.title: Misclassification of cited prior work on bias auditing
comments.quote: In particular, our text-to-video models inherit the challenges faced by prior work on text-to-image models, and our future work will involve auditing for forms of social bias, similar to [6, 7, 50, 12] for image-to-text and image labeling models.
comments.explanation: The sentence categorizes references [6] and [12] as image-to-text and image labeling models, which contradicts the titles provided in the reference list. Reference [12] is explicitly titled dall-eval: Probing the reasoning skills and social biases of text-to-image generative transformers, making it a text-to-image study, not image-to-text. Reference [6] (Gender shades) focuses on facial attribute classification rather than image labeling or generative modeling. This misattribution confuses the methodological lineage the authors intend to cite. Rewrite the sentence to read ...similar to [6, 7, 50, 12] for facial classification, image captioning, and text-to-image generation because references [6] and [12] explicitly address classification and text-to-image tasks, respectively, contradicting the authors' claim that they cover image-to-text and image labeling.

coarse__glm-4.7-flash.label: Coarse (glm-4.7-flash)
coarse__glm-4.7-flash.overall_feedback:
This paper introduces a diffusion model for video generation, extending standard image diffusion architectures to 3D U-Nets with factorized attention. The authors demonstrate state-of-the-art results on benchmarks like UCF101 and Kinetics-600, and present a novel reconstruction-guided sampling technique for conditional generation. While the methodology is sound and the empirical results are strong, the paper lacks a critical analysis of its limitations and a concrete demonstration of the method's practical utility in generating long, high-fidelity videos.

The paper makes a solid contribution by extending diffusion models to video and introducing a practical sampling technique. The empirical results are competitive, particularly on video prediction tasks. However, the paper fails to adequately address the core challenge of generating long, temporally coherent videos, which is the primary motivation for video generation research. The reconstruction guidance method, while theoretically interesting, is not rigorously analyzed in terms of its impact on long-term coherence, and the paper lacks a comprehensive evaluation of its scalability.

Overview issues:

- Missing Analysis of Long-Term Coherence: The paper's primary contribution is video generation, yet it fails to provide a rigorous analysis of the method's ability to maintain temporal coherence over long sequences. While Figure 4 shows a qualitative comparison between reconstruction guidance and the replacement method, it does not provide a quantitative measure of coherence, such as a metric for flickering or temporal consistency. The paper's claim that reconstruction guidance 'outperforms existing replacement or imputation methods' is supported only by FVD and IS metrics, which are not sensitive to long-term temporal structure. A critical omission is a failure to demonstrate that the method can generate videos of length significantly longer than the training window (16 frames) without significant degradation in quality or coherence. This is a major limitation for a video generation paper, as the ability to generate long videos is a key differentiator from image generation models. The authors should add a quantitative analysis of temporal coherence, perhaps using a metric like the temporal FID or a human evaluation study, and provide a clear analysis of the error accumulation in the autoregressive extension process.

- Unjustified Claims About Sample Quality: The paper makes strong claims about sample quality, stating that it achieves 'state-of-the-art results' on various benchmarks. However, these claims are often based on metrics like FVD and IS, which are known to be imperfect for video generation. FVD, for example, is sensitive to global differences in motion and appearance but may not capture subtle temporal inconsistencies. The paper's comparison with previous work is also problematic because it does not consistently report the same evaluation protocols. For instance, in Table 3, the authors mention that their evaluation protocol differs from previous work, leading to slightly different FVD scores. This makes it difficult to assess the true performance of their method relative to the state-of-the-art. The authors should provide a more comprehensive evaluation, including a qualitative analysis of the generated videos and a discussion of the limitations of the metrics used. They should also clarify the differences in evaluation protocols and ensure that their comparisons are fair and apples-to-apples.

- Lack of Practical Feasibility Analysis: The paper proposes a novel reconstruction-guided sampling technique for conditional generation, but it fails to provide a practical analysis of its feasibility. The method requires the model to perform a reconstruction of the conditioning data at each sampling step, which can be computationally expensive. The authors do not provide any analysis of the computational cost of their method, nor do they compare it to other conditional sampling methods in terms of runtime. This is a significant omission, as the practical utility of a generative model is often limited by its inference cost. The authors should provide a detailed analysis of the computational cost of their method, including the number of forward passes required per sampling step and the total time to generate a video of a given length and resolution. They should also compare their method to other conditional sampling methods, such as classifier-free guidance, in terms of both quality and speed. This analysis would help readers understand the trade-offs involved in using the proposed method.

- Insufficient Discussion of Limitations: The paper briefly mentions the limitations of its approach in the conclusion, stating that the methods have the potential for harmful impacts and that the models reflect the biases of their training datasets. However, it fails to provide a more detailed analysis of the technical limitations of the proposed method. For example, the paper does not discuss the limitations of the factorized attention mechanism, which may not capture long-range dependencies as effectively as a fully 3D attention mechanism. It also does not discuss the limitations of the reconstruction guidance method, such as its sensitivity to the weighting factor $w_r$ or its potential for overfitting to the conditioning data. The authors should provide a more comprehensive discussion of the technical limitations of their method, including a sensitivity analysis of the hyperparameters and a comparison with alternative architectures. This would help readers understand the scope of the method and guide future research.

- Missing demonstration of temporal coherence over long sequences: The paper claims to generate long videos through autoregressive extension, yet it lacks a rigorous demonstration that the method maintains temporal coherence beyond the training window of 16 frames. While Figure 4 qualitatively shows coherence for a 64-frame sample, the authors do not provide a quantitative analysis of error accumulation or long-term consistency. For a video generation paper, this is a critical gap. To address this, the authors should compute a temporal metric—such as the temporal FID or a flickering score—for generated sequences of varying lengths (e.g., 32, 64, 128 frames) and compare them against a baseline method. This would empirically validate the claim that reconstruction guidance successfully mitigates the temporal drift observed in the replacement method.

Recommendation: Major Revision. The paper presents a promising extension of diffusion models to video generation and introduces a novel sampling technique. However, the current version lacks a critical analysis of the method's ability to generate long, temporally coherent videos, which is the core challenge in video generation. The empirical results are strong, but the paper would benefit from a more comprehensive evaluation, including quantitative measures of temporal coherence and a discussion of the practical feasibility of the proposed method. The paper would be suitable for a top-tier venue after a major revision that addresses these limitations.

Revision targets:

- Add a quantitative analysis of temporal coherence, such as a metric for flickering or a human evaluation study, to demonstrate the method's ability to generate long, temporally coherent videos.

- Provide a detailed analysis of the computational cost of the reconstruction-guided sampling technique and compare it to other conditional sampling methods in terms of both quality and speed.

- Clarify the differences in evaluation protocols used in the paper and provide a more comprehensive comparison with the state-of-the-art, including a qualitative analysis of the generated videos.

- Discuss the technical limitations of the proposed method, including the limitations of the factorized attention mechanism and the sensitivity of the reconstruction guidance method to its hyperparameters.

comments.title: Unsubstantiated optimization claim
comments.quote: Our model is a natural extension of the standard image diffusion architecture, and it enables jointly training from image and video data, which we find to reduce the variance of minibatch gradients and speed up optimization.
comments.explanation: The abstract claims that joint training 'speeds up optimization' and 'reduces the variance of minibatch gradients,' but it does not quantify these effects. Without reporting the actual speedup factor or variance reduction percentage, this claim is unsubstantiated.

comments.title: Contradiction between introduction and abstract
comments.quote: In this work, we present first results on video generation using diffusion models, for both unconditional and conditional settings.
comments.explanation: The introduction claims the work presents 'first results,' while the abstract claims 'state-of-the-art results,' which is a contradiction. The authors should clarify whether the 'first results' refers to a specific task (e.g., text-conditioned video generation) or if the SOTA claim applies to a different subset of experiments.

comments.title: Temporal coherence claim unsupported by metrics
comments.quote: To generate long and higher resolution videos we introduce a new conditional sampling technique for spatial and temporal video extension that performs better than previously proposed methods.
comments.explanation: The abstract asserts that the new technique 'performs better than previously proposed methods,' yet this claim relies solely on FVD and IS metrics, which do not measure temporal coherence. To substantiate this, the authors should provide a quantitative analysis of temporal consistency—such as a temporal FID or flickering score—for sequences of varying lengths.

comments.title: Missing evaluation details for FID metric
comments.quote: We evaluate our models using standard metrics such as FVD [54], FID [19], and IS [43]; details on evaluation are provided below alongside each benchmark.
comments.explanation: The text mentions using FID but fails to specify the feature extractor or reference dataset required to reproduce the reported scores. The reader cannot verify if the FID scores correspond to the standard ImageNet FID or a dataset-specific FID.

comments.title: Discrepancy in reported TGAN-F FID score
comments.explanation: The reported FID score of 8942.63 for TGAN-F contradicts the value of 7817 reported in the source paper [26] for the same model and resolution. The authors should verify the source of the 8942.63 value and either correct the table or provide a citation for the specific experimental setup that produced this score.

comments.title: Ambiguous comparison of model performance
comments.quote: In Table 1 we present perceptual quality scores for videos generated by our model, and we compare against methods from the literature, finding that our method strongly improves upon the previous state-of-the-art.
comments.explanation: The text claims to 'strongly improve' upon the state-of-the-art, but Table 1 shows the proposed method (FID 295, IS 57) performs significantly worse than TGAN-v2 (FID 3431, IS 26.60) at the same 16x64x64 resolution. The authors should clarify which model they consider the state-of-the-art and provide a fair comparison based on the same resolution and dataset.

comments.title: Ambiguous definition of Inception Score
comments.quote: For FID and FVD, we report two numbers which are measured against the training and validation sets, respectively. For IS, we report two numbers which are averaged scores across 1 split and 10 splits of samples, respectively.
comments.explanation: The definition of the two numbers reported for IS is ambiguous; it is unclear whether this refers to the number of samples per split (e.g., 1 sample vs 10 samples) or the number of random splits of the dataset (e.g., 1-fold vs 10-fold cross-validation). This ambiguity makes it impossible to reproduce the reported Inception Score.
